skill.name: animejs
skill.description: Anime.js adapter patterns for HyperFrames. Use when writing Anime.js animations or timelines inside HyperFrames compositions, registering animations on window.__hfAnime, making Anime.js seek-driven and deterministic, or translating Anime.js examples into render-safe HyperFrames HTML.
skill.summary: Anime.js adapter patterns for deterministic, seek-driven animation in HyperFrames video compositions.
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: All 28 static analysis findings are false positives. The 22 'shell backtick execution' alerts are Markdown code formatting backticks (inline spans and code fences), not Ruby shell execution. The 3 'hardcoded URL' alerts point to legitimate CDN library URLs and a documentation reference. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'system reconnaissance' alerts are documentation text misidentified by pattern matching. The SKILL.md is a standard documentation file with no executable code, malicious patterns, or prompt injection attempts.

low_findings.title: Markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution
low_findings.description: All 22 external_commands alerts correspond to Markdown backtick characters used for inline code formatting and code fences. These are standard Markdown constructs, not Ruby shell backtick execution. The SKILL.md file contains no executable shell commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation examples
low_findings.description: Three URLs are present in the SKILL.md file: two CDN URLs for loading the Anime.js library (cdn.jsdelivr.net) and one reference link to the official Anime.js documentation. These are legitimate resource URLs shown in code examples and a credits section. The skill does not make network requests itself.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm false positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged line 3 for a 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. Line 3 contains YAML frontmatter description text describing Anime.js adapter patterns. No cryptographic algorithm is present.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance false positive
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged lines 16 and 95 for 'system reconnaissance'. Line 16 contains documentation advice about avoiding DOM mutations based on wall-clock time. Line 95 is a markdown section header reading '## Avoid'. Neither is reconnaissance activity.

content.user_title: Create seek-driven animations with Anime.js in HyperFrames
content.value_statement: Anime.js animations use wall-clock time by default, which breaks video rendering in HyperFrames. This skill provides the contract, code patterns, and validation steps to make Anime.js animations seek-driven and deterministic on the HyperFrames clock.
actual_capabilities: Generate Anime.js animations with autoplay disabled and register them on window.__hfAnime
Create Anime.js timelines with controlled sequencing and offset timing for multi-step animation
Use ES module imports of Anime.js with seek-driven adapter compatibility
Translate existing Anime.js examples into render-safe HyperFrames HTML
Validate compositions using npx hyperframes lint and npx hyperframes validate
Animate multiple independent micro-animations in a single shared registry
limitations: Cannot handle animations that rely on DOM mutations inside callbacks or event handlers
Does not support infinite loops or non-deterministic timing sources like wall-clock time
Requires manual registration of each animation on window.__hfAnime; no auto-discovery support
Not suitable for complex multi-scene sequencing; GSAP is the recommended path for those cases

use_cases.title: Animate SVGs in a HyperFrames video scene
use_cases.description: Use Anime.js to animate SVG elements with transforms and opacity in a seek-driven video composition. Register each animation on the HyperFrames clock for deterministic playback.
use_cases.target_user: Motion designers working with vector graphics

use_cases.title: Port existing Anime.js demos to HyperFrames
use_cases.description: Take existing Anime.js examples from CodePen or documentation and adapt them for deterministic, clock-driven rendering in HyperFrames by adding autoplay false and proper registration.
use_cases.target_user: Developers migrating web animations to video

use_cases.title: Create micro-animations for UI elements
use_cases.description: Add small decorative flourishes to multiple UI elements using Anime.js compact syntax and push them to the shared window.__hfAnime registry for synchronized playback.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers adding polish to video compositions

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic seek-driven Anime.js animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write an Anime.js animation for HyperFrames that animates a target element. Set autoplay to false, define finite duration and easing, then register the animation on window.__hfAnime.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding a simple entrance animation to a composition element

prompt_templates.title: Build a multi-step Anime.js timeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Anime.js timeline with three steps for a HyperFrames composition. Each step should animate different targets with offset timing. Set autoplay to false on the timeline and register it on window.__hfAnime.
prompt_templates.scenario: Sequencing multiple animations that build on each other

prompt_templates.title: Convert a CDN Anime.js example to HyperFrames
prompt_templates.prompt: Take the following Anime.js animation and adapt it for HyperFrames: add autoplay false, register on window.__hfAnime, remove any callback DOM mutations or randomness, and ensure finite durations. [paste example]
prompt_templates.scenario: Porting existing Anime.js code from the web into a video composition

prompt_templates.title: Set up an ES module Anime.js animation with validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write an ES module script block for HyperFrames that imports animate from the Anime.js ESM build, creates a seek-driven animation, registers it on window.__hfAnime, and includes comments explaining how to run npx hyperframes lint and npx hyperframes validate to confirm the composition is correct.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a production-ready composition with module imports and validation

output_examples.input: Animate a loading spinner SVG using Anime.js in HyperFrames
output_examples.output: The skill generates an HTML composition with a script block that creates a rotation animation on an SVG spinner element. Autoplay is set to false, the animation has a finite duration and easing curve, and the instance is pushed to window.__hfAnime so HyperFrames controls playback timing.

output_examples.input: Create a title reveal with a staggered underline animation
output_examples.output: The skill produces a timeline with two steps: first the title text fades and slides up, then an underline bar scales horizontally after a 250ms offset. Both steps share an easeOutCubic easing. The timeline is registered on window.__hfAnime for seek-driven playback.

best_practices: Always set autoplay to false on every Anime.js animation and timeline to prevent wall-clock conflicts with the HyperFrames seek adapter
Register animations immediately after creation using window.__hfAnime.push() and avoid deferred registration inside callbacks, promises, or event handlers
Use finite duration and repeat values computed from the composition duration instead of infinite loops or default repeat values
anti_patterns: Leaving autoplay at the Anime.js default, which causes animations to run on their own clock instead of following HyperFrames seek position
Using anime.running auto-discovery instead of explicit window.__hfAnime.push() calls, resulting in animations not being found by the adapter
Creating animations inside timers, async callbacks, or event handlers, which breaks deterministic initialization and seek-based playback

faq.question: Why must I set autoplay to false for Anime.js in HyperFrames?
faq.answer: Anime.js runs on its own internal clock by default. HyperFrames needs full control over time to support seeking. Setting autoplay false lets HyperFrames advance animations with instance.seek() instead.

faq.question: Does this skill work with both CDN and ES module builds of Anime.js?
faq.answer: Yes. The HyperFrames adapter works with any Anime.js build as long as the returned object exposes seek(), pause(), and play() methods. Import or load Anime.js however you prefer.

faq.question: How do I validate that my Anime.js composition is correct?
faq.answer: Run npx hyperframes lint and npx hyperframes validate on your composition. These commands check for common issues like missing autoplay false or unregistered animations.

faq.question: Can I use Anime.js callbacks in HyperFrames?
faq.answer: Avoid callbacks that mutate the DOM based on wall-clock time, network state, or unseeded randomness. These side effects break deterministic rendering and may produce different results on each frame.

faq.question: When should I use Anime.js instead of GSAP in HyperFrames?
faq.answer: Use Anime.js for small SVG and DOM flourishes where its syntax is more compact, or when porting existing Anime.js examples. For complex scene sequencing, GSAP is the recommended HyperFrames authoring path.

faq.question: How do I animate multiple elements independently with this skill?
faq.answer: Create separate anime() calls for each element, set autoplay false on each, and push every instance to window.__hfAnime. The adapter will seek all registered instances together.
